section_title: INTRODUCTION TO THE ICT ASIAN KILL ZONE
content:
Welcome to this teaching on the ICT Asian Kill Zone, the first of several specific times of the day I like to
look for setups. Admittedly, this is the least active time for me, living on the East Coast of North America
(New York time). This module will focus on the following ICT concepts:
- The importance of time and price
- The Asian open
- The Asian session
- Important characteristics that make up the Asian session

- IDEAL PAIRS AND OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRIES
The Asian open is a time when the Australian Dollar, New Zealand Dollar, and Japanese Yen pairs are
ideal, as their markets are active. This small window of opportunity can perfectly fit North American traders
looking for a part-time gig after a 9-to-5 job, or even serve as the sole foundation for a trading model,
without needing to trade London or New York sessions. The Asian open can sometimes set up an optimal
trade entry (OTE) pattern that offers 15 to 20 pips for a scalp. While a higher time frame bias is helpful,
short-term retracements in either a bull or bear market can offer similar OTE setups.

section_title: IDENTIFYING THE ASIAN OPEN ON CHARTS
content:
Using a 15-minute timeframe on a platform like Forex Ltd (my demo account), pressing Ctrl+Y will
delineate what I refer to as the Asian open with a vertical line. This specific timing will fluctuate relative to
daylight savings time, but for the most part, I look at a window of about two hours, so it doesn't have a great
impact. What I look for is a move that originates or forms immediately after the Asian open. An example
with the New Zealand Dollar showed an OTE coming down with one singular candle, giving an OTE,
running equal highs to the previous day's range, and ultimately running up to the previous Friday's high.
This entire move was done during the Asian session, prior to the Frankfurt or London open.

During periods when the market is not willing to run in the Asian session in other words, there isn't an
optimal trade entry pattern it will consolidate until midnight New York time. The Asian trading session
actually extends beyond midnight New York time, but I focus specifically on the realm from the Asian open
to midnight. The setup or information I glean from these hours prior to midnight will be covered in future
tutorials. The importance of the consolidation holds many clues to how the daily range will develop over the
remainder of the 24-hour trading day. For example, the Euro Dollar and Pound Dollar generally aren't very
active during the Asian session. When they are not creating a significant high or low or initiating a run off
an OTE, this Asian session can be viewed as a consolidation that will be very meaningful for our analysis in
future lessons.

- CROSS PAIRS AND VOLATILITY
The pairs that are not coupled with the dollar index, referred to as crosses, are most active during this time
of day. The US Dollar tends to consolidate until midnight New York time, which frees crosses like Aussie
Yen, Pound Aussie, and many other pairs that cross with the busy currencies of this time of day. While I
personally do not like to trade the Asian session as these are my 'family hours,' I am not trying to
discourage you from researching it, as it may become your niche. If you are a working-class hero and
cannot watch the markets during the day, London, or New York sessions, you can trade these crosses in
the evening hours before midnight and still get restful sleep. Many times, you can catch anywhere between
20 and 50 pips, not every day, but a couple of times a week.

section_title: CROSS PAIR TRADING EXAMPLES AND BENEFITS
content:
With this understanding, we can look at an example like the Aussie Yen, showing a beautiful optimal trade
entry sell that runs 20 pips below the New York session lows, a perfect symmetrical 20-pip sweep, and a
beautiful Fibonacci extension overlay. If you are time-limited in the States, you can supercharge the Asian
session by using cross pairs. You can still get movement in pairs like the Aussie Dollar, Dollar Yen, and
New Zealand Dollar, but you're going to experience a drag sometimes because of the Dollar Index. You get
much more volatility by having a pair coupled with a cross like a Euro Aussie or a Pound Swiss, where
there is a lot more volatility because of its cross nature and it's not being held back or limited by way of the
Dollar Index.

section_title: ASIAN OPEN TO MIDNIGHT NY TIME
content:
Speaking of the Pound Swiss, you can see this one here gives a beautiful example of a bullish optimal
trade entry that trades above the previous day's high and reaches a target on the Fibonacci. These trades
form prior to London's volatility and outside the scope of New York. So, over the evening hours on the North
American continent, there are about 2, sometimes 3, hours where you can really dial in and get a setup.
Many times, it's going to come in the form of a cross, anything not coupled to a dollar but has a pair that's
usually active during the Asian session, which are the Aussie, New Zealand, and Yen pairs. Their countries
are active, their markets are open, and even though they are smaller markets, they do start the financial
day. We agree that the financial day starts at midnight New York time, as the dollar is still the reserve
currency. The sweet spot is that small window of opportunity between the Asian open and midnight for
crosses. During this time, the dollar is usually quiet, so we're not going to see a lot of manipulation by way
of the dollar. All the movement is going to be done by using the crosses. If you look for a strong currency
against a weak currency during this time of day and get an optimal trade entry pattern, you'll often get a
wonderful opportunity to trade during a typical slow period of the 24-hour banking cycle.

Hopefully, you found this teaching insightful. For more information like this, you can find it at
innercircletrader.com or my YouTube channel, inner-circle trader. Until next time, I wish you good luck and
good trading.
